1.synthesis: Fourteen years later, Paul went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, taking Titus along. He went up by revelation and presented to them the gospel he preaches among the Gentiles—privately to those who seemed influential—lest somehow he was running or had run in vain (Galatians 2:1–2). But Titus, who was with him, was not compelled to be circumcised, though he was a Greek (Galatians 2:3). The matter arose because of false brothers who slipped in to spy out the freedom they have in Christ Jesus in order to bring them into slavery—to whom Paul did not yield in submission even for a moment, so that the truth of the gospel might be preserved for them (Galatians 2:4–5). Those who seemed to be something added nothing to him. Rather, seeing that Paul had been entrusted with the gospel for the uncircumcised, as Peter with the gospel for the circumcised, and recognizing the grace given to him, James, Cephas, and John—the pillars—gave him and Barnabas the right hand of fellowship: Paul to the Gentiles and they to the circumcised. Only they asked that Paul remember the poor, which was the very thing he was eager to do (Galatians 2:6–10).
1.historical_context: This second Jerusalem visit (vv.1–10) is widely identified with the Jerusalem Council of Acts 15 (c. 49 AD), though some date it to the famine-relief visit of Acts 11:30. The private meeting 'with those of reputation' (v.2) before any public session is diplomatically wise: Paul tests his gospel with the Jerusalem leadership before any public confrontation. Titus as a Greek Gentile who was not circumcised (v.3) is the test case that the Jerusalem meeting resolved—the pillar apostles did not require his circumcision. The 'false brothers' (pseudadelphoi, v.4) who infiltrated the meeting are Paul's term for those who advocated circumcision-requirement for Gentile believers—a consistent Jewish-Christian rigorist position. The handshake (dexiai, right hands of fellowship, v.9) is the formal ancient gesture of partnership and covenant; the 'pillars' (James, Cephas, John) acknowledge the parallel mission structure: Paul-Barnabas to Gentiles, the Jerusalem apostles to Jews. The only condition: remembering the poor (v.10)—the Jerusalem collection that dominates 2 Corinthians 8–9 is here authorized as Paul's apostolic obligation.
1.literary_structure: The second Jerusalem visit (vv.1–10) completes the positive case for Paul's apostolic independence while establishing the Jerusalem leadership's endorsement of his Gentile mission. The structure moves from the visit's occasion (revelation) through the test case (Titus not circumcised) to the confrontation with false brothers (resisted) to the pillar apostles' endorsement (right hand of fellowship). The 'those who seemed to be something added nothing to me' (v.6) is Paul's careful positioning: not disrespecting the Jerusalem leaders but demonstrating that the meeting was mutual recognition, not subordination. The 'only they asked us to remember the poor' is Paul's signal that the Jerusalem agreement was modest, practical, and uncontested—the opponents' claim that Paul needed to add circumcision was not the Jerusalem leadership's position.
1.theological_application: Paul's resistance to the false brothers 'for a moment' (v.5) is described as necessary 'so that the truth of the gospel might be preserved for you'—the pastoral implication is stark: to yield on circumcision even once, even under pressure, would have conceded that Christ's self-giving is insufficient and that Gentile believers need an additional qualification. The 'freedom we have in Christ Jesus' (v.4) that the false brothers wanted to bring into slavery is precisely the freedom from Torah-observance as the condition of covenant standing—a freedom Paul will develop in chapters 3–5 as the gift of the Spirit and the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise. The church must identify and resist the 'false brothers' in every generation who introduce additional requirements for full standing before God—not from suspicion of all tradition but from commitment to the sole sufficiency of Christ.
1.christology: The parallel mission structure—'gospel for the uncircumcised' and 'gospel for the circumcised' (v.7)—does not imply two different gospels but one gospel with two missionary directions: the same Christ proclaimed to two different audiences. The 'grace given to Paul' (v.9) that the pillars recognize is the risen Christ's own commission and empowerment—the same grace that effected Paul's transformation from persecutor to apostle. The right hand of fellowship is the formal recognition that Paul's Gentile mission and the Jerusalem Jewish mission serve the same risen Lord and proclaim the same gospel. The 'one who worked in Peter for the circumcised worked also in Paul for the Gentiles' (v.8): the risen Christ is the one agent of both missions, working through different apostles in different fields but with the same transforming power.

11.synthesis: When Cephas came to Antioch, Paul opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned. Before certain people came from James, Peter was eating with the Gentiles; but when they came, he drew back and separated himself, fearing the circumcision party. The rest of the Jews acted hypocritically along with him, so that even Barnabas was led astray by their hypocrisy (Galatians 2:11–13). When Paul saw that they were not walking in step with the truth of the gospel, he said to Cephas before them all: 'If you, though a Jew, live like a Gentile and not like a Jew, how can you force the Gentiles to live like Jews?' (Galatians 2:14).
11.historical_context: The Antioch incident is the most dramatic confrontation between two major apostles in the NT. Peter had been eating with Gentile believers at Antioch—a significant social act implying that his previous Cornelius conviction (Acts 10–11) had led him to abandon the Jewish table-fellowship restrictions that would have separated him from Gentiles. When the 'people from James' arrived (probably Jewish Christians from Jerusalem concerned about Jewish-Christian reputation in Jerusalem, where maintaining Jewish practice was politically important), Peter withdrew, fearing criticism from 'the circumcision party.' The withdrawal was not merely a personal preference but had communal consequences: it implied that the Gentile table was impure and that full fellowship required Gentiles to observe Jewish food laws—effectively requiring Gentile Judaization. Barnabas, Paul's trusted partner, was swept along, which signals how powerful the social pressure was. Paul's public confrontation (kata prosōpon, 'to the face') was necessary precisely because the behavior was public and its implications communal.
11.literary_structure: The Antioch incident (vv.11–14) is the narrative climax of the autobiographical defense (1:11–2:14) and the transition to the theological argument (2:15–6:10). It demonstrates that even the pillar apostle can act contrary to the truth of the gospel under social pressure—the gospel is not safe in any human hands, however respected. The incident also provides the immediate occasion for the justification-by-faith argument that begins in v.15: Paul's rebuke of Peter becomes the frame for the letter's central theological exposition. The 'walking in step with the truth of the gospel' (v.14) is Paul's criterion: gospel-fidelity is not merely doctrinal but behavioral, enacted in the social practices of table fellowship.
11.theological_application: Peter's hypocrisy reveals that the pressure to Judaize can affect even those who have had the clearest divine confirmation of Gentile inclusion (Acts 10: the Cornelius vision) when social and relational costs are high. The 'fear of the circumcision party' (v.12) that drove Peter's withdrawal is the sociological engine of the Galatian crisis: the agitators' pressure works through social belonging and ostracism, not merely theological argument. Paul's public response is not merely about Peter's behavior but about the gospel's social logic: if the risen Christ has made Jew and Gentile one body, then the social separation of table fellowship contradicts the gospel's social reality. 'Walking in step with the truth of the gospel' (v.14) means that community practices must correspond to gospel claims—the church's social life is a form of theological statement.
11.christology: The question 'how can you force Gentiles to live like Jews?' (v.14) is ultimately a christological question: if the risen Christ has already accomplished the inclusion of the Gentiles through his death and resurrection (as the Cornelius episode demonstrated), then any practice that re-introduces the Jew-Gentile boundary denies the sufficiency of what Christ has done. Peter's table withdrawal implicitly re-erects the 'dividing wall of hostility' that Christ's body abolished (Ephesians 2:14). The social practices of the community are christological claims: a table that separates Jew and Gentile proclaims a Christ whose cross did not break down the wall; a table where all eat together proclaims the Christ who made the two one. Behavioral hypocrisy is therefore doctrinal error about Christ's work.

15.synthesis: Paul continues his address to Peter (or now turns to the Galatians): 'We ourselves are Jews by birth and not Gentile sinners; yet we know that a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ, so we also have believed in Christ Jesus, in order to be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, because by works of the law no one will be justified' (Galatians 2:15–16). If, in seeking to be justified in Christ, they are found to be sinners too, is Christ then a servant of sin? Certainly not! For if he rebuilds what he tore down, he proves himself a lawbreaker (Galatians 2:17–18). 'For through the law I died to the law, so that I might live to God. I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. And the life I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me' (Galatians 2:19–20). He does not nullify the grace of God—for if righteousness were through the law, then Christ died for no purpose (Galatians 2:21).
15.historical_context: The phrase 'works of the law' (erga nomou) is Paul's term for the specific Torah-observances (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath) that marked the boundary between Jews and Gentiles—the social and religious practices that constituted covenant membership in Second Temple Judaism. Paul's claim that 'no flesh will be justified by works of the law' echoes Psalm 143:2 ('no one living is righteous before you'), which Paul applies to the impossibility of self-justification through Torah-performance. 'Faith in Jesus Christ' (pistis Christou, vv.16, 20) may be either 'faith in Christ' (objective genitive: our believing in him) or 'the faithfulness of Christ' (subjective genitive: his faithful obedience to death)—the theological point converges: justification is from Christ's side, not ours. 'I have been crucified with Christ' (v.20) is Paul's compressed account of baptismal union with Christ's death and resurrection (cf. Romans 6:3–4): the old 'I' who lived under the law's condemnation has died; the new 'I' lives by the faith/faithfulness of the Son of God.
15.literary_structure: Verses 15–21 pivot from the narrative of the Antioch incident to the theological argument of the letter. The justification claim (v.16) is the letter's central thesis, stated three times in a single verse for emphasis. The hypothetical objection (v.17: if we Gentile-livers are sinners, is Christ a sin-servant?) is refuted (v.18: rebuilding the law-wall proves one a lawbreaker, not Christ). The 'I' of vv.19–20 is both autobiographical (Paul's own experience) and representative (every believer's union with Christ). The closing syllogism (v.21: if law gives righteousness, Christ died for nothing) states the only alternative to grace: either Christ's death accomplished justification, or law-observance does—one cannot have both, because accepting the latter denies the former.
15.theological_application: 'I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me' (v.20) is perhaps Paul's most compressed account of the Christian life: the old identity constituted by Torah-performance, ethnic boundary markers, and self-justification has been crucified; the new identity is constituted by union with the risen Christ who lives in and through the believer. This is not the erasure of personal identity but its transformation: the 'I' that lives by faith in the Son is still 'I'—but this 'I' is defined by its relation to the living Christ rather than by its own performance. The life of faith is the life of one who has accepted the verdict of the cross on the old self and received the life of the resurrection as the new self's animating principle. To add Torah-observance as a condition of justification is to rebuild the very structure the cross demolished.
15.christology: 'The Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me' (v.20) is one of the most personal christological statements in Paul's letters: the cross is not merely a cosmic event but the personal act of the Son's love for the individual believer. 'Who loved me' names the motive; 'gave himself for me' names the act. The same formula appears in Ephesians 5:2, 25 applied to the church collectively—here it is radically particularized: the Son's self-giving is for 'me,' the individual—not in opposition to its cosmic scope but as its personal realization. The Son of God who gave himself for Paul is the same Son who was revealed in Paul (1:16), who is now alive in Paul (2:20)—the entire Galatian argument is a series of unpacking moves from this single christological reality: the risen Son lives in those for whom he gave himself, and their life is his life in them.
